text: i am going to be real for a moment here and tell you what i do not want to tell you. give up. you have already given it your best shot. you read the books that you needed too and not just one 2 1/2 of them (don't feel bad about half of wind up bird chronicles, that book is long and he really had not moved away from being a short story writer to developing into a novelist, which i think he first does in hard boiled wonderland and the end of the world, though also i think he comes to maturity with kafka on the shore). as for one with interesting characters, that is really hard to say, since well you already read kafka on the shore which had a pimp cornell sanders, i mean come on that is some cool shit. and he really is into down on their luck, depressed characters whose lives are not at first extraordinary until they start talking to demigods. the issue of translation is a good one, and i am sure that the writing would be more rich in its original language (for instance one short story of his called something like on some april day i met the 100% perfect woman, in that japanese the title suggests both the meeting and never meeting of said perfect woman). so, here are some questions for you: do you like the traditional magical realists? (garcia marquez, borges, calvino) if so stick to them, they are already so concerned with translating that their words can speak across languages, if not go for that shit, and eat your fucking fill, it is like crack, literary crack. also, maybe try his short stories? i really like blind willow sleeping woman, some of those stories brought chills all over my body. but then again i often feel alienated, derealized, and quite depressed, melancholic, and find myself drawn towards writing about absence and that essential gap. really, don't feel bad (i am sure you don't really, but the sentiment still exists) not all writers are for everyone, maybe murakami just is not your style, and that is ok, in fact i think it is fucking awesome. and if all else fails try the other murakami the ryu version, though he may have suffered even more in translation, and is really only good if you like horror movies.

the major reason that the us leads in maternal mortality is not related to the number of in-hospital births, but instead is due to the fact that many mothers with co-morbid conditions have poor care prior to pregnancy. contrast this with european countries, where every citizen has access to health care. you don't see as many poorly controlled type two diabetic mothers in these countries. it's not that american mothers are dying because they give birth in-hospital, but rather that the european women are healthier on average. i'd like to see universal health care (not *insurance*) before they send women to give birth at home.

yes, in part the name came from a misinterpretation of their name, though many believe that this was a *purposeful* misinterpretation. in his letters back to spain and his description of the peoples, he emphasized their "violent" nature, though most scholars believe that he only did this due to wanting to create an excuse to get at the natural resources in the area. by labelling them as monsters who eat each other, he justified the church coming in to help convert the heathens and also labelled them as sub-human, justifying their enslavement. this is a well accepted interpretation of events by many anthropologists. > two days after his "conversation" with the "caribe" he says: "there is a great deal of copper on the island of caribe and on matinino, but it would be difficult to obtain it in caribe because the people eat human flesh" (174). he even makes the extraordinary claim: "i can see the island of caribe and have determined to go there, since it is on the course to the island of matinino, which is inhabited only by women" (174). however, on january 16th as he leaves the new world, columbus discovers that the island of caribe remains tantalizingly out of reach, and he finally has to give up the search for the man-eaters: "i was compelled to abandon the course that i believe was taking me to the island; i returned to the direct course for spain... 11 (177). >the island of caribe, like its fierce man-eating inhabitants, exists only in columbus's imagination. but for the old world, his words defined the reality of the "otro mundo." his language created reality, his fiction became fact, his words gave birth to the man-eating cannibals/caribes --the ferocious natives of the new world. >during february, 1493, on his homeward journey, columbus writes a letter reporting his extraordinary discoveries. this letter will quickly circulate throughout europe and serve as the first definition of the new world. near the end of the letter, he summarizes what he has found. he writes that he didn't find the normal monsters people expected, like one-eyed men and people with dogs' heads, but he did discover some even more exotic monsters-the caribes. he says: "thus i have found no monsters, nor had a report of any, except in an island 'carib,' which is the second at the coming into the indies, and which is inhabited by people who are regarded in all the islands as very fierce and who eat human flesh" (jane 200). >why does columbus include among his wonderful discoveries the caribes and their island, a people he did not meet and an island he did not visit? a few lines later in the letter he lists the future gifts from the new world he will bring to the king and queen: gold, spices, cotton, mastic, aloe, and perhaps the most valuable gift of all--"slaves, as many as they shall order, and who will be from the idolaters." who are "the idolaters"? not the good indians he has met, who, as he says earlier in the letter, "do not hold any creed nor are they idolaters." the idolaters are, of course, the man-eating caribes. http://home.roadrunner.com/~jhartzog/cannibals.html http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/columbus_letter_on_the_first_voyage

we moved onto a 4 acre lot with 4 mobile homes and a camper on it in the mountains of northern new mexico 20 years ago. my dad became good friends with the landlord, who lived in the camper. old man didn't have any close family and only a couple of fishing buddies who came out every now and then. pancreatic cancer eventually took his ability to do anything alone and my father helped him for a couple of months with chores and whatnot. he died and left my father, a tenant, with everything he owned except for his camper, which went to one of the fishing buddies. my dad didn't know about this until after his death and we went from renting to owning our own home and 3 others, a tractor, two vehicles, and a workshop/wellhouse packed with tools and supplies. these things happen. edit: phrasing

you are simply talking about .05% of the league community. also, you can not sit here and claim that you don't get angry with the game. whether you are lying to me or yourself, you know for a fact you can not say that. playing blind pick or normal draft is not stressful and therefore are usually pleasant games. the toxicity comes when doing solo que and even riots records will show this. you say that everyday you deal with stress and this is in fact true. in this situation though you are dealing with real people and if not dealing with people then yes you do rage, and clinch your fist and scream/ do whatever one does when feeling this way. it is normal for every person to do this and in no way am i saying you can solve this problem. there are anger management courses for a reason, because they are well needed. league promotes this kind of attitude not only because it is competitive but because of the time you put into it and the results you get. you may disagree but it really is the case. imaging going outside and having to dig a foundation for a building in your back yard. then before you are finished it starts raining and you can not finish, you wake up the next morning only to find some of the mud has been washed back onto the base of the foundation you have already been trying to dig out. this is an analogy of how the solo que system works and is the main issue with toxicity in league of legends. they say that only 2% of the community is toxic, and out of that 2% 99% of the toxicity comes from solo que. it is simply math, and riot needs to face the problems head on instead of trying to dance around it.

i too have had a torsion. for me i didn't notice anything until i woke up from slumber around 3am or so i don't remember the actual time. i wore jeans to bed like i have many times as a kid. apparently puberty didn't enjoy this. i woke up with this crazy pain. i went to take a hot shower thinking it'd relax my manhood and things would return to normal. oddly enough my girlfriend at the time and i were talking about the possibility of a guys balls getting twisted up. then soon after that convo i saw the adventure bros episode that was centered around testicular torsions. it was like my destiny to have my fucking nuts contorted like some circus act. going to the hospital was terrible. the doctor put me up on display to interns "see how his balls are fucked up, yup thats what a torsion looks like". "how fascinating doctor!" this doctor couldn't even do the surgery on me because i was a minor, still had to pay the bills though... so i go on a magical ambulance ride and i notice the paramedics whispering over to my grandfather who took me at the time "w-what'd he do to himself". this was a continually mortifying experience. people thinking i'm some weirdo who gets off on messing up my own family jewels. before surgery i wasn't scared at all. i guess i figured this must happen. i was extremely calm the entire time for some reason i had faith things would go right. maybe it was the drugs all i know was i just wanted the pain to end. im glad the anesthesia took me out so fast i didn't like the room i was in at all. very bleak, the room just screams "we're going to cut into your body". after surgery i remember waking up thinking "are... are they still there?" no one fucking told me i just remember being really lucid. i think the worst part was going to school with this nut support thing on bobbling around in pain because of the stitches. i couldn't come up with a lie to tell people why i was hobbling so much so being a kid i just told the truth when people wouldn't leave me alone. i figured my story would help others avoid the issue. i still had both of my balls but that didn't stop the kids from saying i had one ball. even my uncles would be so cruel to joke with me "did they give you a nice glass ball"? i coulda stayed home on pain medication but oh no i had to be a good student... the doctors said i was a genius for being able to self diagnose myself right away at such a young age and save my precious organs. so there's that.

you know...i sometimes wonder if people with different forms of cancer tell each other that their cancer is worse than the other's cancer? i can just imagine that: "my lung cancer is so much harder to deal with than your breast cancer because ____."

>don't do this. just go as sole prop and get your hands dirty. especially if you're just developing a web tool or a niche site, the only reason you'd want to start an llc right away is for liability reasons, which your don't have to worry about. . *edit: i should clarify. the work being outsourced to other people is already being done, without needing the money for it (upfront). i know them and they're helping me more because of being my friends and having the skills i need, helping me to get out of this rut i've been in for over a year with disability, etc. the work on my mvp i get for free, future work i will pay them for.* . i would've gone this road, as you (and i suspect, /u/localcasestudy/) suggest i do. however, and please do read me out here. my computer died a month ago. as a photographer, having a computer - especially a somewhat powerful one - is vital, and currently that tool is broken. all that i do on a pc now, is on a borrowed machine and i will be leaving norway (my home country) to go overseas to usa & canada for 6 months, starting middle of may. any work on this project will at that point require me to have a laptop in order to continue working on it. . i also need every single penny i currently have + whatever i make from here till then for rent, food, transport, etc, etc, when i'm travelling and trying to make a living doing photos while working on my business. in order to cut back costs on travelling and replacing my (dead) desktop home computer, i can take the $3,500 (30,000 nok) required to open a company (you only have to put up the capital, it doesn't actually get spent - the new company immediately has it all available for expenses) and pay for these things through the company, as opposed to out of my own pocket. i will then be able to write off all these expenses on my business, and even apply for vat cash-back on up to 20% of overhead expenses through a project run by our equivalence of the irs. the laptop alone, due to requirements posed by my photography part of worklife, will require $1,200+ of the $3,500 (of which i am $700 short, 2 weeks until this money comes in), and if i simply buy it *now* then i will most likely not be able to afford everything i need for the timeline i have set to reach my business' sub-goals by the deadlines set. this does not mean i am not working on the business daily, however. i am acquiring personnel to do for me the tasks i would need a lot of time to do adequately myself. these are not strangers, but people i know dearly and whom are skilled at their respective jobs (which is exactly what i need them for in my case, too, so they are helping a friend out for compensation - i trust their professionalism), enabling them to complete at a much higher level of skill and in a much shorter time-frame. the funds required for having the actual work is minimum, but when it comes to purchasing required pre-made software that has all of the functionalities (or most) of what i want my mvp to include, i have to put aside an additional $300-500. in terms of the total $3,500, it will also cost me $900 to have an account verify the value of said items if i just buy them as a sole prop now, and try to transfer/sell them to my company down the line. $900 that i will never have to spend if i simply setup the company structure and bank account, and purchase everything through there. >i would not recommend software or a complicated website to anyone that doesn't know how to code my website in itself is not all that complicated, but the market is large and currently does not have anything other to offer than individual web-shops. this despite of being a prime candidate for a multi-vendor market in which a large selection, variation of high quality goods, competitive prices, ease of purchase/delivery and everything else will work in favour of a relatively large and thus far under-served customer base. like i said, most my competitors are web-shops (and physical outlets), so i will be the first online marketplace launching for this industry/demographic. >my question to you would be: is there a way you can solve the same problem in this market in a way that doesn't scale? no, not really. the high quality vendors that are doing well already have their individual physical and digital shops. during relevant events, there are also frequent physical marketplaces that take place where more of the (usually local) hobbyist/tiny "word of mouth" vendors show up with some of their (also high quality) goods. the biggest value i will be creating for all of these vendors, along with the buyers, is being the first to offer a digital marketplace that makes it easy for vendors big and small to compete on equal terms. it will also take multiple, small fragmented physical marketplaces that are currently not even present online, and provide them with a free and well equipped business front to reach not just more of their local clientele, but also expand to potentially reach a global customer base. furthermore, as this market is so under-served and -developed, the products you usually find in the shops are somewhat... non-diverse and boring at times, when the hobbyists and small-timers are bringing such exciting new ideas to the table. a lot of people that don't even show up to the events in person will all of a sudden be presented with a level of work they're not even aware exists. so no, i think it'll be both a much more challenging and improbable approach to solve this problem in a non-scaleable way, as one of the main selling points of the product i'm offering to my market is the ease of scaling for everyone involved. my $1 revenue will be through commission of having successfully connected a buyer with a seller. thanks for such a long and helpful post! :)

i read a book once (apologies that i don't remember the title) about a hippie spiritualist that went to an ashram in india in the sixties and met with a guru there. he claimed the guru asked him about all the psychedelics he spoke of, out of genuine curiosity. he said he showed the guru six doses of lsd, which the guru promptly took before he had a chance to explain the potency. apparently, hours later, the guru told the hippie he had experienced no change in how he saw the world--the idea being that the lsd unlocks something for normal people that he was able to see on his own. i have no idea about any of this but i thought it was an interesting story.

i did not address this from your prior reply but i meant to > probably because echo europe/amsterdam > /etc/timezone is considerably faster than whatever dropdown menu they can come up with? that is not all you do to set up timezone in arch. to set up timezone in arch you need to follow sections 'locale' and 'time': https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/beginners'_guide#locale there is really no excuse for this nonsense. > any distribution that is going to hardcode partition layout and not give the user a choice in this will never be taken seriously in any remotely professional or serious setting. except that every distribution gives you choice to partition your hard drive in case you need to, and you know this. i am not saying that there should not be provision for special cases. i am saying that not automating where you can for 99% of people is stupid. not only does arch not automate where it would make sense to, but it uses this lack of common sense features to sell itself as "distribution for experts" which makes it snobbish yet retarded. > and arch doesn't cater to people that would just 'go with the default', that's the point. they don't put it in because virtually no arch user would ever use it. it's wasted effort. arch is designed with people in mind who give a damn about what kind of partition layout they want. not true. arch is sold, not to tiny minority of users with special needs, but as mana from heaven that will turn young boys and girls into stylish movie hackers. most people who install arch have an ordinary pc with a single hard drive. > ehh, excuse me? you constantly interact with your partition layout. no you don't. even if you partitioned hd yourself and edited fstab by hand, then afterwards you should not have to worry about it and it should be totally transparent to you. if you muck around with partition tables and fstab daily than you are wasting time. > the system can't guess how you want to layout and fstab your hdd vs your ssd, if you want a shared boot partition with another operating system installed or not, again, those all special cases that all distributions allow for. if you want to run windows along side of linux (and many people do), or if you have more than one hard drive than yes you may want to muck about with fstab briefly. but... > if you want /home or /usr/local on their own partitions shared with other systems or not, if you want a specific backup partition, how large you want your root filesystem to be and what filesystem you want it to be in. that is the computing equivalent of blowing yourself. why would you run more than one linux os where they share /home? and why would you backup data on the same drive in a different partition? this is exactly what i meant when i said "computing for it's own sake" what non-computing goal could there be for doing something like this? > i'm sorry but i very much interact with the fact that i have my partition layout arranged in such a way that large data such as music and images goes onto the hdd and all shit that is not large but needs to be read from quickly goes onto the ssd, my os performance would be kind of dismal without this. i installed mint and accepted default for partitioning, i am running everything in one large partition and i do not have ssd drive. according to you, my system performance must be abysmal, right? truth is if my system was partitioned to get any bit of extra-efficiency, i would not even notice. if you used your more efficient system for a century, you would still not make up the time you spent in creating it. > yeah, or i'm doing it because i don't want to wait an hour and a half for videogames loading because they load of an hdd instead of an ssd really? 90 minutes to load video game from a hard drive? >> but the problem with that is that you could end up not having something that you need. > in which case you will notice it instantly and install it. so removing things you don't need is a total hassle, but figuring out just what you need and installing it is a total breeze? > it is far easier to notice you don't have something you need than notice you have something installed you don't nede which you forgot about, obviously. you are assuming that you know exactly what you are missing and how to install it, which is a big assumption on your part. however, what usually ends up happening is that you end up having to learn how linux does something internally, just to be able to burn cds. how can that possibly be easier and more efficient than having a feature that most people want to use, just working by default? > you think debian and ubuntu made the netinstall for shits and giggles? nobody is recommending ubuntu netinstall to others to install a system.

i am going to precede this by saying that i am not any type of medical professional at all. so this is just layman's shit. diabetes really isn't a big precursor of cancer. years ago, there was an advertisement for dow chemical, and their motto at the time was "we don't make the products you love, we make the products better". diabetes is like that. if you have a family history of heart disease, then it enhances that risk, and effect. same with high blood pressure, and things of the sort. but some of the conditions that diabetes itself will cause (some of which can be fatal) are gastroparesis, loss of circulation to the extremities, kidney failure, neuropathy, blindness, and things of the sort. hopefully a medical professional will pop their head in the door, and could explain it better.

where trump and his ethic has been so terrifyingly successful is in the depersonalisation and "othering" of those outside the "norm", ie, poc, queer, immigrant, poor, unemployed, disabled etc. we know from other studies that many who gravitate to his politics are low in empathy, and sometimes education (though that can be a problematic take), but he has taken this latent tendency and fuelled it into a raging bonfire. i have had people tell me, calmly and rationally, they they simply do not feel i have the right to live in *their* country, because i am queer, an immigrant, and in one bizarre case because i have a postgraduate degree and "people like me make him sick". i have been told, calmly and rationally, by members of my own family that i should be sectioned, or put in a camp, and kept away from children. i have been called a scab and a blight on society even though i have worked my entire adult life in some capacity. it doesn't even make me mad anymore; these people feel righteous in their cause, and they cheer when harm befalls anyone who doesn't fit their concept of "normal".

my seizures completely disagree. pot is not only better at managing my seizures than drugs like depakote or topamax, the side effects are much less severe. vaping high cbd concentrates also leaves me much more clear headed than any prescription pharmaceutical ever has. a single hit before bed has cut my number of incidents to a tenth what they used to be. i also plan on trying epidiolex (basically pharmaceutical cbd) if and when the fda ever gets off their asses and approves it. likewise i don’t know anyone who takes it to cure cancer. mostly they take it to deal with the nausea and pain that comes with chemo. it’s not a miracle cure, and yes many people are simply taking it “medically” to get high. but for those who have serious conditions that respond well to it, it’s quite amazing. there’s a lot of us with epilepsy, crohns, ms, and a few other conditions that simply respond excellently to cannabinoids.

reality check, man. not my situation, but i used to work with very poor people who had a job but could not make ends meet with their crappy salary. they couldn't afford medications or physical therapy, they had a crappy insurance that only covered er visits. these people had families and if they didn't get their paycheck on their payday, they will probably not eat until they got it. on top of that, if they don't pay their rent, they'll be evicted. this is only an example of someone with a job and health insurance.i have hundreds of stories of people on disability, with chronic conditions, or people that don't qualify for medicaid, but are still dirt poor. yes, this is usa....

i have to disagree. if someone gets wic for their baby, they get milk, eggs, cheese, cereal, wheat bread, and vegetables. only healthy items. i know my experience with food stamp abusers does not reflect the general public, but there is no excuse to have steak and lobster and energy drinks covered at my expense. if we are providing food to people with no social security number or those without work, they should be required to job search and perform a certain number of community service hours unless they are on disability and receive only healthy items. we need to start to do something about the health of our nation and any little bit helps. and having limited shoulder movement and being bipolar, getting disability because "it hurts" or being depressed is absolute bullshit. i work 60-80 hours a week to provide for my family. what the fuck happened to people being proud in this country. now everyone wants a handout. "help me because i am sad"... build a bridge and get over it. government benefits need to have work requirements and limitations. just like holding office should. sorry but the era of "being a man" or "being a strong woman" are over. now people that want to play dressup and have gender dysphoria are paving the way for a politically correct nightmare. everyone should work for what they have and stop telling everyone that their feelings are more important than everyone else's.

my mother is a nurse, working in a not so well off area, and has multiple stories just like this. she has recurring patients losing toes and feet because apparently they would rather have fast food and sweets than have a foot or mobility. they're never old, they're typically around 50s or 60s. they do have insulin, they just choose not to use it or monitor their blood sugar levels. it's like someone getting a completely treatable cancer and going "eh i could go get chemo today but i'd rather go see a movie instead" it's not just a slice of cake, a snack, a cheat meal. it's the reaper flipping a coin. heads you lose a toe. tails you lose your life.

i don’t know where you are however in the us aa is ubiquitous and for someone feeling the negative effects of addiction having somewhere to go immediately could be the difference between life and death. depending where you are there could be meetings 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. depending on your financial situation or if you have health insurance, you may have access to therapists that specialize in treating addiction and there are many treatment options. unfortunately there is no silver bullet for treating alcoholism and addiction. aa is a place where you can meet men and women who have struggled with the same issues. addiction is a lonely debilitating affliction. most non alcoholics don’t understand why we do what we do to ourselves, family, and society. aa is first and foremost a support group and like therapy it is not a quick or even good fit for most people. it was founded during a time when they treated epileptics with prayer because they didn’t have the science to understand what caused it. you are at the atheism reddit so if you are not a god person aa will be a real tough thing to grapple with. it was founded on religious principles. i am an atheist as well as a member of aa. i was an atheist long before i came into the rooms of aa and almost 30 years later am still in aa and still an atheist. for me making a connection with people that suffered from the ravages of addiction who were living happy, useful, productive lives was a revelation to me. that connection helped me feel less alone and provided guidance as well as support to do other work to uncover the real reasons for my destructive drinking and drugging. i wish you well.

i remember in the military, training for mass casualty events, the first people you wanted to treat were not the most "fucked". they were the people who had life-threatening injuries that might be survivable if they got immediate treatment. people who had injuries with very low odds of survival were not treated first, because there was no point on wasting time on someone who was almost certainly going to die. like, if someone's had their brains leaking out all over the place, there is not much you can do for them anyway and they're probably going to die but if someone else has a partially amputated limb, a punctured lung, or an obstructed airway, you might be able to make a big difference in their survival chances if you treat them first.

your story is very similar to my 3yo son. he was diagnosed at 10 months with polymicrogyria causing left hemiplegia. like you, he also had a rough entrance into the world at birth. before we found the pmg, we also thought the cp was caused by ischemia at birth. now we know that my wife contracted cytomegalovirus (cmv) during pregnancy which caused the brain malformation pmg on his right brain. cp is the result of that malformation in the form of left hemiplegia. so from what i understand... brain damage, or a brain malformation in utero, that causes some type of life long physical paralysis would in fact be cp. the cp umbrella is pretty much, a cerebral issue that causes any type of palsy hope this helps.

23m nonsmoker sclc chiming in. sclc is highly agressive which in turn means chemo starts off very effective. since he manages the side effects well and there's no paraneoplastic disease you guys should have quite some time at decent quality of life to get things sorted out. of course you're right regarding survival rates. regrettably medicine has not advanced that much in sclc care during the past 20 years. however, palliative care did, and your father will enjoy higher quality of life even as his health eventually declines. some things that may help him: * the most important thing is keeping his spirits up. i'm not talking about the "stay positive, it's going to be alright, you're a fighter / warrior / whatever" stuff but making him feel his life worth living. once people stop leaving their bed or at least using their head things go downhill. of course you're supposed not to overdo it when suffering from fatigue, but consider taking walks with him (depending on his health). * talk to him a lot. relive memories and get him a bunch of framed pictures. he loves his family, seeing them happy will make him happy. let him vent, cry, laugh, express himself. he wanted to be a great dad and the way you talk about him he succeeded, tell him! * consider getting psychooncologic staff involved. they help patients and their loved ones cope and have plenty experience doing so. most hospitals with a cancer ward have them. * food tends to be bad in hospitals and chemo makes things worse. multiple small but high-energy meals can help prevent weight loss. consider bringing a small gift for the nurses. if they're feeling happy that'll be nice for your dad, too. * encourage him to ask the docs to change up medication that doesn't work. there's dozens of anti-nausea and pain management options available. * allow him as much freedom as possible. cancer makes you feel helpless. try not to force your values or wants on him so that he may express himself freely. of course everyone's different. your father is probably from another country and more than three times as old as i. so make sure to ask him what will help and think about his personality and past to work something out. you know him better than anyone on this sub. finally, do proper self-care. dealing with cancer is hard for everyone involved. i certainly noticed when people got uncomfortable or felt unable to cope.
also he isn't using a handicap walker anymore. imagine that.

i only had a month in rehab after a month in icu. and it took about a year of teaching myself and watching youtube videos of how other paralyzed ppl were doing things to get to where i could just care for myself fully. my pt after rehab was trash and we barely did anything there that gave me anything additional to use in my arsenal. she had a really great... ahem, posterior so i kept going until my insurance cut my pt and ot both about 7 months in. from that point i was already transferring and driving and dressing myself thanks to youtube. so i have been able to work out a lot more. i'm glad i'm not a heavy guy but i've still lost a lot more weight and definition than i can be happy with. such is life. i'm trying to get a standing frame and off road wheelchair next. but nothing is ever going to be easy or effortless again. i'm 2 years in as a t6 complete now and still feel trapped by circumstance a lot. but i also finally feel capable of being semi self sufficient again. but no i did not get that from my rehab facility. when i left i could barely transfer. was still hooked on heavy pain meds, couldn't do a dip to do pressure relief and to top it off my turtle shell brace they made me wear for 3 months was giving me a sore on my spine that wound up going straight to bone before i was able to get a good skin treatment team in a hospital after discharge. i had begged my staff at the rehab facility to have me refitted for a new brace but they refused citing insurance and whatever else. i left with a very bitter taste in my mouth and it wasn't until i met other people with sci in chairs that i realized what things were still possible. like swimming and the sort. the movie murderball actually taught me a ton about what we are still capable of and i saw some things in it that gave me some ideas of techniques i should use to make things easier in my own life, it's on prime and i recommend it. sorry this is all over the place now so i'll end it here. tldr: no i don't think rehab set me up fully for going back into the world. but through strength of will, good people in chairs around me, youtube, and a few movies i've learned a lot and now feel a lot more confident in my abilities to survive.

are you aware that autistic people often mean no harm but have no clue what they said was inappropriate or offensive? other types of impairment can create this obliviousness as well (e.g., mental disorders that cloud awareness or cause involuntary actions, an abusive childhood that normalized a bad behaviour to the point where they don’t realize it’s not normal.) someone from another culture can behave inappropriately in the eyes of those outside their group. heck, even having a really bad day can make people say something shitty. you’re living in a bubble if you assume that everyone is innately aware of every social rule in every setting. shunning people under the assumption that they deliberately chose not to be nice discriminated against those who need a head’s up about social or cultural rules, and deprives them the opportunity to learn / improve. i personally provide an explanation to each person with whom i chose to cut ties, and address inappropriate behaviour directly, under the assumption that *not everyone knows what they did wrong and why it’s wrong.* if they don’t improve after receiving a wake-up call explanation, i let them know “you’re doing x again. i’ve had enough. goodbye.” i’m not forcing criteria on others. i would expect anyone with an iota of compassion and fairness not to take gossip at face value. usually, rumours are spread out of malice, not well-intentioned whistleblowing, particularly if done in public. that is slander. the mods ought to have examined the accusation. a photo of the asian female op holding a piece of paper with their username would suffice to dispel the rumour of a white male troll. op was bullied, then permanently banned over a lie despite never breaking a rule or behaving badly. talk about blaming the victim. as regards my dictator remark, certain mods do go on power trips. they enjoy having unlimited control in the fantasy world of the internet way too much. maybe they feel powerless in real life and this is their outlet. they are forcing their unfulfilled needs on the innocent. mods are guardians of a community. in a way, they are a court of law online. if you have an ethical sense of justice, you don’t sentence someone to a subreddit’s death penalty (the permaban) over a rumour. come to think of it, dictatorship isn’t the best analogy in op’s situation. this is more of a medieval witch hunt scenario. an accusation of witchcraft from a spiteful villager was enough to stain them as ‘guilty’ and get them burned at the stake. no investigation required. disheartening that this pattern of punishment is repeating on reddit of all places.

so, you say you're not adhd, but it sounds to me like you very well may have it. adhd is highly genetic, your kid gets it from somewhere. it doesn't necessarily mean that either of and adhd kid's parents have adhd, but it does mean it runs in their family. many adults are being diagnosed in recent years, and it's not because they've suddenly developed it; that's just not a thing, you're born with it as it's a neurodevelopmental disorder. the only reason so many of us weren't diagnosed younger is because the understanding of adhd wasn't very good back then. we now know that it's not just hyper little boys, and also that they won't grow out of it when they get older. the closest we get to growing out of it is developing coping strategies to help us stumble through life. you took the adderal and felt, i imagine, relatively "normal." it made you able to function the way an adult is expected to, yes? that's a good indicator. as for how i got on vyvanse, i have always known adhd was very likely, especially having had it suggested by teachers and a counsellor when i was in school. i figured i was fine since i was getting through life...barely. i just assumed it was that hard for everyone. i finally decided that i was tired of stumbling through life, and had been learning a lot more about adhd, which lead me to realise just how much it was impacting my life. it was so much more than i thought. so, i went to a doctor and asked for an assessment. they referred me to a psychiatrist. i can't afford the extensive assessment, so had a free one done that checked me for basically every condition out there, and the psychiatrist decided i just had anxiety and depression, which do cause adhd-like symptoms. he also said i was a smart kid, so adhd was far too unlikely. so clearly he was still on the old belief that it's a learning disability, which it's not. i did try treating the anxiety and depression for nearly a year before deciding that my anxiety and depression are mostly cause by the adhd, not the other way around. so, basically like what you're going through right now, which is good because you've already gone through the steps of trying antidepressants first. venlafaxine made my anxiety go away, but it made everything else go away too. i was not okay with the apathy. wellbutrin helped and continues to help, but not very much. i still had all the adhd symptoms, which lead to feeling anxious and depressed. i decided to go for a second opinion, started seeing a new mental health provider, and she not only agreed that i was right to seek a second opinion, but she was surprised the previous psych couldn't tell just from seeing and talking to me. she picked out a psychiatrist for me and sent in the referral. he is an amazing psychiatrist. he also agreed i've likely got it during our first appointment and started me on stimulants right away. we still haven't finished with the diagnostic process, because i don't have a few grand for the specialised assessment, but he's treating me for it in the meantime. and let me tell you, it has been life changing. he also can give the diagnosis without the expensive assessment this way, we just have to wait until i've been medicated long enough and notice all the changes that should come with medication. so far, especially since increasing my dose last week, it's absolutely doing what it should for an adhd brain. my advice for you and your journey to get diagnosed, do not tell any medical professional that you tried adderal. accident or not, they do not like to hear that. just tell them that your kid has adhd and you've been learning a lot about the condition, which has lead you to start noticing a lot of the symptoms in yourself. tell them you *feel* you have adhd, not that you think it - this was the advice of my mental health nurse. be prepared to give examples of your struggles. if the first psychiatrist disagrees with adhd but you still feel you have it, seek a second opinion. vyvanse has been shown in studies to work on treatment resistant depression, so even if you don't have adhd, it is possible to get it prescribed. you'd just have to have failed with a lot of antidepressants before a doctor might consider giving it to you off-label. i do think you probably have adhd though.

cancer might be difficult to diagnose - we had the same problem with mum. several locums, all prescribing drugs to counter side-effects of the current prescription for several weeks. turned out she had spinal cancer. difficult to diagnose, fair enough, but why hadn't they at least done an x-ray? stitch in time etc. a friend was in the merchant navy. he kept going to their medics with blinding headaches. they fobbed him off with painkillers and eventually accused him of being workshy. one friday afternoon, he'd called in at the health centre for some help and was told to come back on monday. a brand new doctor, not due to start till the monday, commented "he'll be dead by then!" turned out he had a brain tumour - benign fortunately - but they very nearly killed him with their indifference. same happened to my husband. 5 different medics kept fobbing him off. it was my pharmacist friend who, just on hearing his symptoms and without the benefit of a list of his meds, exclaimed "but those are classic signs of sodium deficiency!" they'd literally overmedicated him to half an inch of his life. ye of little faith? that's me!

if you're at the point where you need to leave altogether, and you need to get unemployment, your best bet is to get yourself let go. take all the sick days you need, and allow your health problems to affect the quality of your work when you're there (ex. if you're having a 40% day, give 40% instead of trying harder and having to recover all weekend). if you can't work, let it show and it will get you let go before too long. if this is leading to you possibly needing disability, start documenting everything now. including, if you can get it, any reason for termination. "too many sick days to be employed" may help your ssdi case later. also, consider filing now- if you get better you can cancel, but if you don't you will be glad you filed earlier. i regret that at my last real jobs i let them ask me to leave voluntarily and just kinda slunk away without trying to fight and unemployment. it could have made a big difference, being so unable to work and so ill from pushing too long, i was homeless and penniless within a few months. i also regret not filing for disability 6 years before i finally did.
